terms.definition: An AI system that doesn't just answer — it takes actions in a loop: it can call tools, read the results, and decide the next step toward a goal.
A process where an AI decides some of its own steps dynamically (rather than following a fixed script) to reach a goal. More flexible than a plain workflow, but costlier and less predictable.
The broad field of making machines do things we'd call 'intelligent' — from simple rule-based systems to modern neural networks.
The problem of making an AI reliably do what we actually intend — helpful, honest, and safe — especially as it becomes more capable and autonomous.
A way for software to talk to a service over the internet. Using a 'hosted model via API' means you send requests to a provider that runs the model for you.
The mechanism inside a transformer that lets the model weigh how much each word should 'pay attention to' every other word when working out meaning. The breakthrough behind modern language models.
Using AI to make a human faster or better at a task (a copilot), rather than replacing them. Contrast with **Automation**.
Having AI perform a task end-to-end with little or no human involvement. Best for high-volume, well-defined, low-stakes work.
The algorithm that trains a neural network by, in effect, blaming the error on the weights and nudging each one a little to reduce it — repeated over many examples. (You never do this by hand.)
A model fresh from pre-training — knowledgeable but raw. It becomes a useful assistant only after instruction fine-tuning and alignment.
A standard test used to compare models. Useful as a signal, but easily gamed and rarely matches your real task — always evaluate on your own data.
Systematic unfairness in an AI's outputs, usually inherited from skewed training data. A key concern whenever AI makes decisions about people.

How much damage a system can do when something goes wrong. A model that only writes text has a small blast radius; one wired to send money has a large one. Good design keeps it small relative to the stakes.
Splitting documents into smaller passages so each can be embedded and retrieved individually in a RAG system. Chunk size is a major quality lever.
Meeting the legal and regulatory rules that apply to your AI use — e.g. the EU AI Act, privacy law (GDPR), or sector rules in health, finance, and law.
The maximum amount of text (in tokens) a model can consider at once — its short-term working memory. Everything you send plus its reply must fit.
An AI that assists a human in real time (drafting, suggesting, summarising) while the human stays in control and accountable. The 'augment' pattern.
The honest way to compare AI options: total cost divided by tasks completed *acceptably* — not cost per raw request. A cheap call that often fails isn't cheap.
An attack where bad data is slipped into a model's training set or a RAG knowledge base so the system later behaves the attacker's way.
A requirement that data physically stays within a particular country or region, often for legal or contractual reasons. A major driver of local-vs-cloud decisions.
Machine learning using large neural networks with many layers. It powers modern image recognition, speech, and language models.
Training a smaller 'student' model to imitate a larger 'teacher' model, producing a compact model that keeps much of the capability.
Running a model directly on the end device (phone, laptop, camera) instead of in the cloud — strong for privacy, low latency, and offline use, at the cost of using smaller models.
A list of numbers (a vector) that represents the *meaning* of text, an image, or audio, so similar things sit close together. The backbone of semantic search and RAG.
Measuring whether an AI's outputs are good, systematically rather than by impression. The golden rule: evaluate on *your* real tasks, not generic leaderboards.
Giving a model a few examples of the task inside the prompt to guide its output. 'Zero-shot' means giving none — just the instruction.
Further-training an existing model on your own examples so it adopts a style, format, or behaviour. Good for *how* a model responds; use RAG for fresh *facts*.
A large, general-purpose model trained on broad data that can be adapted to many tasks — the 'foundation' others build on.
The most capable general-purpose models available at a given time (e.g. the top Claude, GPT, and Gemini models).
Deep-learning models that *create* new content — text, images, audio, code — rather than only classifying or predicting a number.
The discipline of deploying and operating AI safely, legally, and in your control — covering privacy, security, risk, compliance, accountability, cost, and oversight.
A processor that does many calculations in parallel, ideal for the tensor math AI relies on. GPU memory and cost shape what you can run, especially when self-hosting.
A signal, computed during training, that tells the model which direction to nudge each weight to reduce error. The raw material of learning (handled by the tools, not you).
Tying a model's answer to specific, retrieved source material so it's based on real data rather than memory. RAG is a grounding technique.
Rules and filters placed around a model to block unsafe inputs or outputs — e.g. refusing certain requests or stripping sensitive data.
When a model states something false or invented with full confidence. A core risk to design around (with grounding, citations, and human review), not a bug you can fully remove.
A design where a person reviews or approves an AI action before it takes effect. The standard safety pattern for high-stakes or irreversible actions.
Mixing local and cloud AI — e.g. sensitive or high-volume work on local models, hardest tasks on cloud frontier models. Often the pragmatic best of both.
Running a trained model to get an answer (as opposed to *training* it). This is what you pay for per use, and where latency and cost live.
Software that runs a model you host yourself and serves responses efficiently (e.g. vLLM for production, Ollama for local, llama.cpp for edge).
Fine-tuning a base model on examples of instructions and good responses, teaching it to be a useful, directions-following assistant.
Tricking a model into ignoring its safety rules through cleverly worded prompts. A form of direct prompt injection.
Whose laws govern a piece of data. Sending data abroad can subject it to foreign laws and government access — a key governance consideration.
How long the system takes to respond. Critical for live experiences like voice agents; less so for batch jobs.
Giving each user and each AI system the minimum access, tools, and permissions needed for its job. The single most important security principle for AI.
A model trained on vast text that predicts and generates language — the engine behind ChatGPT, Claude, and similar tools.

Lightweight fine-tuning methods that train a small 'adapter' on top of a frozen model instead of the whole thing — far cheaper and faster than full fine-tuning. QLoRA also compresses the model to fit modest hardware.
Systems that learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed with rules (e.g. spam filters, fraud detection, recommendations).
An open standard for connecting models to tools and data sources in a uniform way — a 'universal connector' that reduces bespoke integration code and lock-in.
An added layer that lets an AI persist information across a task or across sessions (facts, preferences, history), usually via retrieval. The context window alone is not memory.
A model design that routes each input to a few specialist sub-networks ('experts') instead of using the whole model every time — big total capacity for less compute per query.
Sending each request to the most appropriate model — cheap/fast models for easy tasks, powerful ones for hard tasks — to control cost and latency.
A model that handles more than one type of data — e.g. text plus images, audio, or video. Check both what it accepts and what it produces.
The engine of deep learning: layers of simple units connected by weights, which learn by adjusting those weights to reduce error over many examples.
Running software and models on hardware you control, inside your own environment, rather than a third party's cloud — strong for data residency and confidentiality.
A model whose weights you can download and run (and often fine-tune) yourself — e.g. Llama, Qwen, Gemma, Mistral. Trades some peak capability for control and privacy. Check the licence.
Coordinating the moving parts of an AI system — chaining model calls, tools, and steps. Agent frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen) live in this layer.
When a model learns its training data too closely and fails to generalise to new, real inputs. A reason 'bigger/more training' isn't always better.
The learned numbers (weights) inside a model. Parameter count is a rough proxy for capability and cost; for MoE models, 'active' parameters matter more than 'total'.
The first, hugely expensive stage of building a model: it reads enormous amounts of data to absorb language, facts, and patterns, producing a raw base model.
The text you send a model — your instructions plus any context. Crafting it well (clear goal, context, examples) strongly affects the output.
An attack where malicious instructions are hidden in content the model reads (a web page, document, or email) to hijack its behaviour. The signature security risk of AI systems.
Shrinking a model by storing its numbers at lower precision, so it runs faster and on cheaper hardware, usually with a small quality trade-off. Enables local inference.
Giving a model relevant, up-to-date, or private information at answer time by retrieving matching passages and adding them to the prompt — 'open-book mode' for an AI.
A second pass in retrieval that reorders candidate passages by true relevance before they reach the model — one of the cheapest ways to lift RAG quality.
A model that spends more compute 'thinking' (working through steps) before answering, doing better on hard, multi-step problems at higher cost and latency.
Deliberately attacking or stress-testing an AI system to find vulnerabilities (e.g. prompt injection, jailbreaks) before real attackers do.
Whether an action can be undone. Reversible actions are safe to automate; irreversible ones (deleting data, sending money) deserve a human gate.
A common alignment method that tunes a model using human preferences to make it more helpful, honest, and harmless.
The observation that making models bigger — more data, parameters, and compute — improved performance in a smooth, predictable way. The engine of the AI boom.
Finding information by *meaning* rather than exact keywords, using embeddings. The retrieval engine inside RAG and recommendations.
Staff using unsanctioned AI tools (often with sensitive data) outside official policy. Best reduced by providing a good sanctioned option, not just rules.
A clear description of what to build — goal, inputs/outputs, constraints, and what 'done' looks like — that lets an AI tool build the right thing. The highest-leverage thing a non-coder produces.
Data generated by AI to train or improve other AI. Useful for filling gaps and protecting privacy, but risks quality decay and bias if over-relied on.
Background instructions given to a model to set its role and behaviour. Helpful for steering, but not a security control — determined prompt injection can get around it.
A setting that controls how random or 'creative' a model's output is. Lower = more focused and consistent; higher = more varied.
A container for numbers arranged in a grid of one or more dimensions. Everything in a neural network — inputs, weights, outputs — is a tensor, which is why AI needs GPUs.
Spending extra computation when answering (rather than only during training) so a model can 'think longer' and solve harder problems — the idea behind reasoning models.
The unit a model reads and writes — roughly a word-piece (about ¾ of a word). Costs and context limits are measured in tokens, not words.
A model's ability to call external functions or services (search, a database, an API) to get information or take action beyond generating text.
The expensive process of teaching a model from data by adjusting its weights. Distinct from *inference*, which is using the finished model.
The neural-network architecture (introduced in 2017) behind essentially all modern language models. Its key idea is attention.
An ordered list of numbers — a point in space. An embedding is a vector; closeness between vectors means similarity of meaning.
A database built to store embeddings and quickly find the nearest (most similar) ones. The retrieval engine behind most RAG systems.
Being so dependent on one provider (via proprietary APIs, data, or deep integration) that switching is costly. Mitigated with abstraction layers and exportable data.
Building software by loosely prompting an AI and accepting what looks right, without rigorous review. Fine for prototypes; risky for production, security-, or money-critical systems.
The set of learned numbers inside a model — its 'knowledge', stored as large tensors. Training adjusts the weights; inference uses them frozen.
Connecting apps to run a defined, predictable sequence of steps automatically (often no-code), increasingly with AI steps inside. Tools: n8n, Make, Zapier.
An AI that builds an internal model of how an environment works — cause and effect — so it can simulate, predict, and plan beyond next-word prediction.
Asking a model to do a task with no examples — just the instruction. Contrast with few-shot, which includes a few examples in the prompt.
